name: atooms

metadata.name: atooms
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: GNU General Public License v3 (GPLv3)
metadata.summary: A framework for simulations of interacting particles

documentation:
Atooms
======

[![pypi](https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/atooms.svg)](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/atooms/)
[![version](https://img.shields.io/pypi/pyversions/atooms.svg)](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/atooms/)
[![license](https://img.shields.io/pypi/l/atooms.svg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GNU_General_Public_License)
[![Binder](https://mybinder.org/badge_logo.svg)](https://mybinder.org/v2/git/https%3A%2F%2Fframagit.org%2Fatooms%2Fatooms/HEAD?labpath=docs%2F)
[![DOI](https://zenodo.org/badge/DOI/10.5281/zenodo.1183301.svg)](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.1183301)
[![pipeline](https://framagit.org/atooms/atooms/badges/master/pipeline.svg)](https://framagit.org/atooms/atooms/badges/master/pipeline.svg)
[![coverage report](https://framagit.org/atooms/atooms/badges/master/coverage.svg)](https://framagit.org/atooms/atooms/-/commits/master)

**atooms** is a high-level Python framework for simulations of interacting particles, such as molecular dynamics or Monte Carlo.

This is the core package: it provides a consistent interface to the basic objects of particle-based simulations. [Feature packages](Feature packages) are built on top of it and implement complex simulation methods and analysis tools.

Quick start
-----------

Here is a small example for setting up a mixture of two types of particles, A and B, in a periodic elongated cell. The number density is set to unity.
```python
from atooms.system import System

system = System(N=64)
system.replicate(times=4, axis=0)
system.composition = {'A': 128, 'B': 128}
system.density = 1.0
```

Particles in the central part of the cell get a random displacement and are folded back into the simulation cell
```python
import numpy

for p in system.particle:
    if abs(p.position[0]) < system.cell.side[0] / 4:
        p.position += 0.5 * (numpy.random.random() - 0.5)
        p.fold(system.cell)
system.show('ovito')
```

![](https://framagit.org/atooms/atooms/-/raw/master/snapshot.png)

Simulation data are stored in trajectory files, which are easy to manipulate and convert with atooms. Here, we write the system species and positions in a single-frame trajectory file using the [xyz format](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/XYZ_format).
```python
from atooms.trajectory import TrajectoryXYZ

with TrajectoryXYZ('input.xyz', 'w') as th:
    th.variables = ['species', 'position']  # actually, this is the default
    th.write(system)
```

The trajectory file can now be used to start a simulation using one the available [simulation backends](https://atooms.frama.io/atooms/tutorial/simulations.html) or your own code.

Features
--------

- Focus on a simple and expressive interface
- API refined over the years towards consistency
- Modular and extensible design via namespace packages
- Semantic versioning - for what is worth
- Easy to interface: in-house codes and custom formats are first-class citizens
- Support for efficient simulation backends, with a focus on GPU codes

Documentation
-------------
Check out the [tutorial](https://atooms.frama.io/atooms/tutorial) for more examples and the [public API](https://atooms.frama.io/atooms/api/atooms) for more details.

Org-mode and jupyter notebooks are available under [docs](https://framagit.org/atooms/atooms/-/blob/master/docs/). You can run them interactively on [Binder](https://mybinder.org/v2/git/https%3A%2F%2Fframagit.org%2Fatooms%2Fatooms/HEAD?labpath=docs%2).

Installation
------------
From the python package index
```
pip install atooms
```

From the code repository
```
git clone https://framagit.org/atooms/atooms.git
cd atooms
make install
```

Contributing
------------
You are welcome to contribute to this project! Please have a look at [these guidelines](https://framagit.org/atooms/atooms/-/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md).

Feature packages 
------------------
Atooms is modular: it is easy to add new functionalities, and just those you actually need.

Feature packages are available from the [atooms main repository](https://framagit.org/atooms). They are installed in the `atooms` namespace to prevent name clashing. If you want to add your own feature package to the atooms namespace, structure it this way
```bash
atooms/your_package
atooms/your_package/__init__.py
```

where ```__init__.py``` contains

```python
from pkgutil import extend_path
__path__ = extend_path(__path__, __name__)
```

Install `your_package` and you are ready to go
```python
import atooms.your_package
```

Authors
-------
Daniele Coslovich: https://www.units.it/daniele.coslovich/

Thanks go to Francesco Turci and Geert Kapteijns for their contributions.

modules.name: atooms.trajectory.factory
documentation:
Abstract factory class for trajectories.

The callable `TrajectoryFactory` returns the appropriate trajectory
class based on either explicit rules or suffix euristics. The list of
available trajectory classes can be updated at run-time using the
`update` method.

Examples:
--------

- Reading an hdf5-format trajectory via the factory

    Trajectory = TrajectoryFactory()
    t = Trajectory('input.h5')

is equivalent to

    t = TrajectoryHDF5('input.h5')

- Ask for a specific trajectory format

    Trajectory = TrajectoryFactory()
    t = Trajectory('input.xyz', fmt='rumd')

This is equivalent to

    t = TrajectoryRUMD('input.xyz')
This file is part of atooms
Copyright 2010-2024, Daniele Coslovich

classes.name: atooms.trajectory.factory.TrajectoryFactory

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.factory.TrajectoryFactory._add
documentation: Atomically add one trajectory class to the factory

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.factory.TrajectoryFactory.add
documentation: Add a trajectory class to the factory

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.factory.TrajectoryFactory.update
documentation: Update factory parsing all trajectory classes from module

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.factory.TrajectoryFactory.register_callback
documentation:
Register a callback `cbk` to be applied when reading a frame.

The callback is registered in the Trajectory instance when
calling the factory, see __call__().
Note: trajectories should implement a method to check if a file is
of their own format or not, to avoid relying on suffix check out
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/456672/class-factory-in-python.
However, one would have to deal with conflicts anyway, so the
settings in update() are fine imo.

modules.name: atooms.trajectory.xyz
documentation: This file is part of atooms
Copyright 2010-2024, Daniele Coslovich

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.xyz._update_radius
documentation: Format callbacks

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.xyz._optimize_arrays
documentation:
Optimize a list of particles properties (`variables`) by
collapsing successive instances of array elements.

Example: ['id', 'x[0]', 'x[1]', 'x[2]'] -> ['id', 'x']

> print(scalars_to_arrays(['pos[0]', 'pos[1]', 'pos[2]']))
> print(scalars_to_arrays(['pos[0]', 'pos[1]', 'vel[0]', 'vel[1]']))
> print(scalars_to_arrays(['pos[0]', 'pos[1]', 'pos[2]', 'hello']))
> print(scalars_to_arrays(['hello', 'pos[0]', 'pos[1]', 'pos[2]']))

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.xyz.TrajectoryXYZ._read_comment
documentation:
Internal xyz method to get header metadata from comment line of
given `frame`.

We assume metadata fmt is a space-separated sequence of comma
separated entries such as:

columns:id,x,y,z step:10
columns=id,x,y,z step=10

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.xyz.TrajectoryXYZ._setup_read_callbacks
documentation: Return a list of callbacks to read the variables given in self.variables

XYZ format with metadata support (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/XYZ_file_format)

It is implemented using a lightweight indexing approach.

classes.name: atooms.trajectory.xyz.TrajectoryNeighbors
documentation:
Neighbors trajectory format

By default, for reading we assume an xyz file with space-separated
integers indicating the particles indices. For writing, we use
comma-separated entries (no space).

modules.name: atooms.trajectory.folder
documentation:
Folder based trajectory.

This is a base class for trajectories that stores configurations as
individual files in a directory. The `dirname` property stores the
directory path; an additional `files` property stores the list of
files sorted by step index.

It supports compressed archives. In this case the input filename is
the path to the archive, which will be decompressed in a temporary
folder.
This file is part of atooms
Copyright 2010-2024, Daniele Coslovich

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.folder.init_folder
documentation:
Initial setup in read mode.

Filename can be a folder, a compressed tar file, a simple file.
Helper functions

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.folder._get_file_steps
documentation:
Return a list of tuples (file, step). The step is extracted from
the file path using `regexp`, which must contain one group for the
step.

classes.name: atooms.trajectory.folder.TrajectoryFolder
documentation: Folder based trajectory.

modules.name: atooms.trajectory.utils
documentation: Useful functions to manipulate trajectories.

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.utils.gopen
documentation: Open a file recognizing gzipped and bzipped files by extension.

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.utils.file_index
documentation: Lightweight file indexing for trajectories via tell/seek

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.utils.split
documentation:
Split the trajectory into independent trajectory files, one per
sample.

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.utils.get_block_size
documentation:
Return the size of the periodic block after which entries in
`data` repeat. It is used to determine the block size in
trajectories with logarithmic time spacing.

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.utils.check_block_size
documentation:
Perform some consistency checks on periodicity of non linear sampling.

`block_size` is the number of frames composing a periodic block.
If `prune` is True, the steps that do not match the first periodic
block will be removed.

Return a new list of steps that match the periodicity.

Example:
-------
steps = [0, 1, 2, 4, 8, 9, 10, 12, 16]
block_size = 4

Note that in this case, len(steps) % block_size == 1, which is tolerated.

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.utils.dump
documentation:
Dump coordinates as a list of (npart, ndim) numpy arrays if the
trajectory is grandcanonical or as (nsteps, npart, ndim) numpy
array if it is not grandcanonical.

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.utils.field
documentation:
Return the field specified by particle attribute `field_name` at a
given `frame`.

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.utils.paste
documentation:
Iterate simultaneously on two trajectories. Skip samples that
exist in one trajectory and not in the other.

Example:
-------
t1 = Trajectory(f1)
t2 = Trajectory(f2)
for s1, s2 in paste(t1, t2):
    pass

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.utils.is_cell_variable
documentation:
Simple test to check if cell changes.

We compare the first frame to an integer number `tests` of other
frames starting from the end of `trajectory`.

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.utils.is_semigrandcanonical
documentation:
Simple test to check if a trajectory is semigrandcanonical.
i.e. if the chemical concentrations fluctuate.

We compare the first frame to an integer number `tests` of other
frames starting from the end of `trajectory`.

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.utils.is_grandcanonical
documentation:
Simple test to check if a trajectory is grandcanonical.
i.e. if the number of particles fluctuates.

We compare the first frame to an integer number `tests` of other
frames starting from the end of `trajectory`.

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.utils.formats
documentation: Return a string with the available trajectory formats.

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.utils.info
documentation: Return a string with information about a `trajectory` instance.

classes.name: atooms.trajectory.utils._PossiblyZippedFile

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.utils._PossiblyZippedFile.__getattr__
documentation: Forward all unknown attribute calls to file stream

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.utils._PossiblyZippedFile.__exit__
documentation: TODO: is this trapped when there is an exception?

Decorator of io stream objects optimized for zipped files.

Reading gzipped files with gzip is very slow.
We use a simpler and faster approach, which is to unzip
the file in a temporary directory and return that file instance.
The temporary file is removed when closing the instance.

Example:

> with ZippedFile('test.gz') as fh:
>     print(fh.read())

modules.name: atooms.system.reservoir
documentation:
Thermodynamic reservoirs to impose temperature, pressure, or
particle numbers in a simulation.

When added to a `System` object, they will determine the statistical
ensemble of a simulation:

    #!python
    system = System()
    system.thermostat = Thermostat(temperature=1.0)
    system.barostat = Barostat(pressure=1.0)
    print system.ensemble == 'NPT'

Note: in an actual simulation backend, these reservoirs will have
additional degrees of freedom, e.g. s and pi in a Nose-like
thermostat.

classes.name: atooms.system.reservoir.Thermostat
documentation: Thermostat to control the temperature during a simulation.

classes.name: atooms.system.reservoir.Barostat
documentation: Barostat to control the pressure during a simulation.

classes.name: atooms.system.reservoir.Reservoir
documentation: Reservoir to control the number of particles during a simulation.

modules.name: atooms.trajectory.ram
documentation: Store trajectory in memory (it can be huge).

classes.name: atooms.trajectory.ram.TrajectoryRam

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.ram.TrajectoryRam._from_data
documentation:
Create a system from a dictionary `data`

The keys of `data` must be valid `what` entries for
`System.view()`.

Store trajectory in RAM

The read_system method of this class conforms with the normal
Trajectory behavior, i.e. a copy of the system is returned when
requesting the same frame multiple times.

classes.name: atooms.trajectory.ram.TrajectoryRamView
documentation:
This class deviates from the normal Trajectory behavior in that it
returns views on the System when calling read_system(), and not
copies. Thus modifications to the read system object will be
propagated to the trajectory.

classes.name: atooms.trajectory.ram.TrajectoryRamLight

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.ram.TrajectoryRamLight.write_system
documentation:
Store copies of the views of the attributes of the System
components
Light-weight version

modules.name: atooms.trajectory.decorators
documentation:
Trajectory callbacks and class decorators.

- "Callbacks" are simple functions that modify the `system` instance
  returned by `read_system()`. They can be registered to `trajectory`
  instance via `add_callback()`.
- "Class decorators" can be used for
  more complex modifications of trajectory behavior.
This file is part of atooms
Copyright 2010-2024, Daniele Coslovich

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.decorators.center
documentation:
Shift the origin of reference frame at the center of the
simulation cell. Particles positions will thus lie between -L/2
and L/2, where L is the cell side along each direction.

This function does not check if that is done multiple times.
Callbacks

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.decorators.change_species
documentation:
Return a modified `system` with particle species changed according
to `layout`.

The possible values of `layout` are:

- 'A': alphabetic, i.e. species are strings like 'A', 'B', ...
- 'C': C-like, i.e. species are integers starting from 0
- 'F': F-like, i.e. species are integers starting from 1

If the current layout already matches the requested one, the
system is returned unchanged.

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.decorators.sort
documentation: Sort particles by species id.

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.decorators.filter_species
documentation: Return particles of a given `species` id.

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.decorators.set_density
documentation: Set density of system to `rho` by rescaling the cell.

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.decorators.set_temperature
documentation: Set system temperature to `T` by reassigning velocities.

functions.name: atooms.trajectory.decorators.fold
documentation: Center and fold positions into central cell.

classes.name: atooms.trajectory.decorators.Sliced

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.decorators.Sliced.__new__
documentation: This is still necessary. slicing via __getitem__ has a large memory fingerprint
since we couldnt write it as a generator (maybe it is possible?)
TODO: adjust uslice to pick up blocks without truncating them
Only return a slice of a trajectory.
Class decorators
Some of these decorators raise pylint errors because of the
lack of visibility on parent methods() or attributes
To properly implement decorators in python see
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/3118929/implementing-the-decorator-pattern-in-python
asnwer by Alec Thomas. if we don't subclass at runtime we won't be able to use the decorated
mathod in other non-subclassed methods.

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.base.TrajectoryBase.close

documentation: Read and return system at frame `index`.

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.base.TrajectoryBase.write
documentation:
Write `system` at a given integer `step`.

If `step` is not provided, it is defined internally by
incrementing by one the last added step, staring from zero.

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.base.TrajectoryBase.read_init
documentation:
Read metadata and/or set up data structures. Need not be
implemented.

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.base.TrajectoryBase.write_init
documentation: Subclass should use it to open files for writing.

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.base.TrajectoryBase.read_system
documentation: Return the system at the given `frame`.
These methods must be implemented by subclasses

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.base.TrajectoryBase.write_system
documentation: Write a `system` to file. Noting to return.

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.base.TrajectoryBase.register_callback
documentation:
Register a callback `cbk` to be applied when reading a frame.

The callback must receive a `System` instance as input an
return the modified `System` instance as output.
Callbacks will be applied to the output of read_system()

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.base.TrajectoryBase.add_callback
documentation: Same as `register_callback()`.

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.base.TrajectoryBase.read_len
documentation: Return the number of frames. Optional.
To read/write timestep and block size sublcasses may implement
these methods. The default is dt=1 and block determined dynamically.

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.base.TrajectoryBase.read_steps
documentation: Return a list of steps.

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.base.TrajectoryBase.read_timestep
documentation: Can be subclassed to parse the timestep.

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.base.TrajectoryBase.write_timestep
documentation: Set the timestep.

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.base.TrajectoryBase.read_block_size
documentation:
Can be subclassed to parse the block size for trajectory with
non-uniform intervals between frames (ex. exponential sampling).

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.base.TrajectoryBase.write_block_size
documentation: Can be subclassed to write the block size.

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.base.TrajectoryBase.variables
documentation: Properties

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.base.TrajectoryBase.copy
documentation: Return a copy of the trajectory

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.base.TrajectoryBase.block_size
documentation:
Return the block size in frames of the trajectory.

A block is a sequence of steps that repeats periodically in
the trajectory. The block size is 1 for trajectories with
constant time intervals between frames. A block size larger
than 1 occurs with exponential sampling, ex. for steps
sequences like 1,2,4,11,12,14,...

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.base.TrajectoryBase.grandcanonical
documentation:
True if the trajectory is grandcanonical, i.e. the number of
particles changes.
Some additional useful properties

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.base.TrajectoryBase.times
documentation: All available times.

methods.name: atooms.trajectory.base.TrajectoryBase.total_time
documentation: Total simulation time.

Trajectory abstract base class.

A trajectory is composed by one or several frames, each frame
being a sample of a `System` taken at a given `step` during a
simulation. Trajectory instances are iterable and behave as file
objects: they can be opened and closed using the `with` syntax

```python
with Trajectory(inpfile) as th:
    for system in th:
        pass
```

alternatively

```python
th = Trajectory(inpfile)
for system in th:
    pass
th.close()
```

To write the state of a `System` to a trajectory, we must open the
trajectory in write mode

```python
with Trajectory(outfile, 'w') as th:
    th.write(system, step=0)
```

Trajectories can use the `variables` attribute to define which system
properties will be written to disk. Concrete classes may thus
provide means to write arbitrary variables to disk via particle
properties. Example:

```python
for particle in system:
    particle.some_custom_property = 1.0
with Trajectory(outfile, 'w') as th:
    th.variables = ['position', 'some_custom_property']
    th.write(system, step=0)
```

To be fully functional, concrete classes must implement
`read_system()` and `write_system()` methods.

`read()` is a template composed of the two following steps:

- `read_init()`: called only once to initialize internal data
structures, grab metadata, etc. Need *not* be implemented by
subclasses.

- `read_system(n)`: actually return the system at frame n. It must
  be implemented by subclasses.

Similarly, `write()` is a template composed of `write_init()` and
`write_system()`. Only the latter method must be implemented by
subclasses.

The `cache` variable reduces access time when reading the same
trajectory multiple times. We use shallow copies to cut down the
overhead. Cache is disabled by default as there is no control on
its size yet.

functions.name: atooms.system.particle.gyration_radius
documentation:
Gyration radius of a list of `particles` in a `cell` with an
optional `weight`.

The optional `center` variable is the index of the central
particle for pbc unfolding. If `weight` is given but `center` is
not, the latter will be the index of the first largest element of
`weight`.

Possible values of `methods` are `N1` (default), `N2` and `min`.

functions.name: atooms.system.particle._lattice
documentation:
Return a list of `N` particles on the sites of a simple cubic
lattice in `d`-dimensional space. A perfect crystalline
configuration is etched so as to match the input value of `N`. The
particles' position are between -0.5 and 0.5 along each axis.

methods.name: atooms.system.particle.Particle.diameter
documentation: Particle diameter.

methods.name: atooms.system.particle.Particle.nearest_image
documentation:
Return the nearest image of `particle` in the given `cell`.

If `copy` is `False`, the particle is transformed into to its
nearest image, otherwise the fucction returns a copy of the
nearest image particle and leave the original particle as is.

If `folded` is True, the coordinates are assumed to be folded
into the central cell (or in a cell just next to it),
otherwise they will be assumed to lie in an arbitrary periodic
cell.

methods.name: atooms.system.particle.Particle.nearest_image_of
documentation:
Return a copy of the nearest image of `particle` from `self` in
the given `cell`.

If `folded` is True, the coordinates are assumed to be folded
into the central cell (or in a cell just next to it),
otherwise they will be assumed to lie in an arbitrary periodic
cell.

methods.name: atooms.system.particle.Particle.distance
documentation:
Return the distance from another `particle`.

If `cell` is given, return the distance from the nearest image
of `particle`. In this case, if `folded` is True, the
coordinates are assumed to be folded into the central cell (or
in a cell just next to it), otherwise they will be assumed to
lie in an arbitrary periodic cell.

If `cell` is None, periodic boundary conditions are *not*
applied and folded is irrelevant.

methods.name: atooms.system.particle.Particle.fold
documentation: Fold self into central cell.

methods.name: atooms.system.particle.Particle.maxwellian
documentation:
Assign the velocity to particle according to a Maxwell-Boltzmann
distribution at temperature `T`.

methods.name: atooms.system.particle.Particle.kinetic_energy
documentation: Kinetic energy.

modules.name: atooms.system.system
documentation:
The physical system at hand.

The system of interest in a classical atomistic simulations is
composed of interacting point particles, usually enclosed in a
simulation cell. The system may be in contact with a thermostat, a
barostat or a particle reservoir.
This file is part of atooms
Copyright 2010-2024, Daniele Coslovich

functions.name: atooms.system.system._canonicalize
documentation:
Given a descriptor string of a System property, return it
in canonical form. This is used to canonicalize viewed / dumped
attributes.

classes.name: atooms.system.system.System

methods.name: atooms.system.system.System.__init__
documentation:
Create a `System` with requested objects.

If provided, `particle` must be a list of `Particle`s.

If `N` is provided and is an `int`, the system is filled with
`N` particles starting from a crystalline template in a
unit-length sided `Cell` in `d` dimensions.

If `N` is a `dict` specifying a target composition, ex. `{'A':
N_A, 'B': N_B, ...}`, the system is filled as above but
setting the species as requested. The assignement is random.

methods.name: atooms.system.system.System.update
documentation:
Update current system attributes in-place using the `other` System
as source.

The default behavior is to make deep copies only of all the
`other` system attributes that are not None, i.e. which are
set.

To overwrite all attributes, even those set in `self` but not
in `other`, use `full=True`.

The lists `exclude` and `only` can be used to avoid updating
some system attributes or to update only some system
attributes, respectively.

This method can be used by subclasses to deal with performace
or memory dellocation issues.

methods.name: atooms.system.system.System.number_of_dimensions

Number of spatial dimensions, guessed from the length of

methods.name: atooms.system.system.System.__view
documentation:
Return a view of system properties specified by `what` as numpy array.

`what` must be a string of the form `particle.<attribute>` or
`cell.<attribute>`. Some aliases are allowed like:

- `pos` (`particle.position`)
- `vel` (`particle.velocity`)
- `spe` (`particle.species`)

If `what` is a System attribute, it is returned.

The requested particle property is set as
a view on the corresponding portion of an internal array. This
allows one to modify the array efficiently and keep the
particle properties in sync. Numpy arrays must be modified
in-place to keep everything in sync.

Particles' coordinates are returned as (N, ndim) arrays if
`order` is `C` or (ndim, N) arrays if `order` is `F`.

Examples:
--------
These numpy arrays are element-wise identical

    #!python
    pos = system.dump('particle.position')
    pos = system.dump('position')
    pos = system.dump('pos')

Collect several properties in a dict

    #!python
    data = {what: system.view(what) for what in ['position', 'velocity']}
Attempt to have a self-contained view method

methods.name: atooms.system.system.System.__dump
documentation: Attempt to have dump rely on view entirely.

methods.name: atooms.system.system.System.dump
documentation:
Return a numpy array with system properties specified by `what`.

`what` must be a string of the form `particle.<attribute>` or
`cell.<attribute>`. Some aliases are allowed like:

- `pos` (`particle.position`)
- `vel` (`particle.velocity`)
- `spe` (`particle.species`)

If `what` is a System attribute, it is returned.

If `view` is `True`, the requested particle property is set as
a view on the corresponding portion of the dump array. This
allows one to modify the dump array efficiently and keep the
particle properties in sync. Numpy arrays must be modified
in-place to keep everything in sync.

If `clear` is True, a new view is created on the requested
particle property.

If `flat` is True, the resulting array is flatted following
`order` using numpy.flatten().

Particles' coordinates are returned as (N, ndim) arrays if
`order` is `C` or (ndim, N) arrays if `order` is `F`.

Examples:
--------
These numpy arrays are element-wise identical

    #!python
    pos = system.dump('particle.position')
    pos = system.dump('position')
    pos = system.dump('pos')
Attempt to have a self-contained dump()
def __dump(self, what, order='C', dtype=None):
assert order in ['C', 'F']
what = _canonicalize(what)
# If the attribute to dump is a plain System attribute, return it
if what in self.__dict__:
return copy.deepcopy(getattr(self, what))
# Extract the requested attribute
assert what.count('.') == 1, f'cannot access nested attribute {what}'
component, attribute = what.split('.')
# Make array of attributes
assert component in ['particle', 'cell']
if component == 'particle':
data = numpy.array([getattr(p, attribute) for p in self.particle], dtype=dtype)
# We transpose the array if F order is requested
if order == 'F':
data = numpy.transpose(data)
data = numpy.asfortranarray(data)
if component == 'cell':
data = numpy.array(getattr(self.cell, attribute), dtype=dtype)
return copy.deepcopy(data)

methods.name: atooms.system.system.System.get
documentation: Alias of `System.dump()`

methods.name: atooms.system.system.System.view
documentation:
Return a view on a system property

This is an alias of `System.dump(view=True, *args, **kwargs)`

methods.name: atooms.system.system.System.species_layout
documentation: Return species layout (A=alphabetical, C=C indexed, F=fortran indexed)

methods.name: atooms.system.system.System.species_layout

System class.

modules.name: atooms.simulation.observers
documentation:
Scheduler and callbacks (aka observers) to be called during a simulation.

To add a callback `func` to a `Simulation` instance `sim` and have it
called every 100 steps

    #!python
    sim.add(func, Scheduler(100))

There are two ways to set them up and add them to a simulation:

1. callable classes

   Example of client code:
     sim.add(simulation.WriterThermo(), 100)
     sim.add(simulation.TargetRMSD(5.0))

2. use functions passing optional *args and **kwargs.

   Example of client code:
     sim.add(simulation.writer_thermo, 100)
     sim.add(simulation.target_rmsd, 100, rmsd=5.0)

To differentiate different types of callbacks, we following a naming
convention (for classes and function). If they contain

- target : these callbacks raise a SimulationEnd when it's over
- writer : these callbacks dump useful stuff to file

Of course, general purpose callback can be passed to do whatever.
This file is part of atooms
Copyright 2010-2024, Daniele Coslovich

functions.name: atooms.simulation.observers._sec2time
documentation:
Convert a time interval in seconds to (day, hours, minutes,
seconds) format.
Helper functions

functions.name: atooms.simulation.observers.write_to_ram
documentation: Write configurations to a trajectory in ram.
Writer callbacks
Callbacks as pure function to distinguish their role we adopt a naming convention:
if the callback contains write (target) in its __name__ then it is a writer (targeter).

functions.name: atooms.simulation.observers.write_trajectory
documentation:
Write trajectory frame from `sim` Simulation instance

The trajectory format is taken from `sim.trajectory_class` and a
local instance with that format is used appending the frames on
successive calls to this function.

If `trajectory` is a Trajectory instance, it is used instead.

functions.name: atooms.simulation.observers.write_thermo
documentation:
Write thermodynamic properties to a file.

By default, available fields are:
- steps
- temperature
- potential energy per particle
- kinetic energy per particle
- total energy
- pressure
- rmsd
- cm velocity norm

The set of available `fields` can be augmented by passing an extra
`functions` dictionary.

The `fmt` dictionary can be used to provide custom formatting
options for fields.

The `precision` parameter controls the default precision of
floating point fields.

functions.name: atooms.simulation.observers._setup_callbacks
documentation: Setup callbacks from `what` list, see `write` for definitions

functions.name: atooms.simulation.observers.write
documentation:
Write generic attributes of simulation `sim` to a file.

`suffix` is a tag appended to `sim.output_path` to define the output
file path. If `path` is provided, however, it is used instead as
output file path.

`what` tells the function what to write and must be a list of either:

- a string representing a valid property of the Simulation instance `sim`

- a tuple `(name, callback)` where `name` is a descriptive name of
the value returned by the `callback`, which is a function that
takes `sim` as first argument

- a string from the following list:
steps
temperature
potential energy per particle
kinetic energy per particle
total energy
pressure
rmsd
conserved energy

functions.name: atooms.simulation.observers.store
documentation:
Store generic attributes of simulation `sim` in the dictonary `data`.

`what` tells the function what to write and must be a list of either:

- a string representing a valid property of the Simulation instance `sim`

- a tuple `(name, callback)` where `name` is a descriptive name of
the value returned by the `callback`, which is a function that
takes `sim` as first argument

- a string from the following list:
steps
temperature
potential energy per particle
kinetic energy per particle
total energy
pressure
rmsd
conserved energy

functions.name: atooms.simulation.observers.target
documentation:
An observer that raises a `SimulationEnd` exception when a given
target `value` of a property is reached during a simulation. The
property is `attribute` and is assumed to be an attribute of
simulation or a function receiving sim as first argument.

Return: the ratio between current and target values of the attribute.
Target callbacks.
They should return a fractional measure of completion

functions.name: atooms.simulation.observers.target_rmsd
documentation: Target the root mean squared displacement.

functions.name: atooms.simulation.observers.target_steps
documentation: Target the number of steps.

functions.name: atooms.simulation.observers.target_walltime
documentation:
Target a value of the elapsed wall time in seconds from the
beginning of the simulation.

Useful to self restarting jobs in a queining system with time
limits.

functions.name: atooms.simulation.observers.target_python_stop
documentation:
Stop the simulation if `condition` is True.

`condition` will interpolate attributes of the passed `sim`
instance. For instance, the condition

    #!python
    {current_step} > 1000 and {rmsd} > 1.0

will stop the simulation when the step is > 1000 and the rmsd > 1.

functions.name: atooms.simulation.observers.shell_stop
documentation:
Execute the shell command `cmd` and stop the simulation if the
command returns an exit value equal to `exit_code`.

`cmd` is actually a format string that may contain references to
the passed `sim` instance. For instance, a valid command is

    #!python
    echo {sim.current_step} {sim.rmsd} >> {sim.output_path}.out

which will append the step and rmsd to {sim.output_path}.out.

functions.name: atooms.simulation.observers.user_stop
documentation:
Allows a user to stop the simulation smoothly by touching a STOP
file in the output root directory.  Currently the file is not
deleted to allow parallel jobs to all exit.

classes.name: atooms.simulation.observers.SimulationEnd
documentation: Raised when an targeter reaches its target.
Default exceptions

classes.name: atooms.simulation.observers.SimulationKill
documentation: Raised when a simulation is terminated by SIGTERM.

classes.name: atooms.simulation.observers.WallTimeLimit
documentation: Raised when the wall time limit is reached.

classes.name: atooms.simulation.observers.Scheduler

methods.name: atooms.simulation.observers.Scheduler.__init__
documentation:
Only one of the arguments can be different from None.

- `interval`: notify at a fixed steps interval
- `calls`: fixed number of notification
- `steps`: list of steps at which the observer will be notified
- `block`: as steps, but will be called periodically
- `seconds`: notify every `seconds`

methods.name: atooms.simulation.observers.Scheduler.__call__
documentation:
Given a simulation instance `sim`, return the next step at which
the observer will be called.

Schedule observer calls during the simulation.

This is nothing but a callable that takes a simulation instance
and returns the next step at which an observer has to to notified.
Scheduler classes

classes.name: atooms.simulation.observers.stuck
documentation: Stop the simulation if the system gets stuck in configuration space

classes.name: atooms.simulation.observers.Speedometer
documentation: Display speed of simulation and remaining time to reach target.

modules.name: atooms.simulation.core
documentation:
Base simulation class with callback logic.

`atooms` provides a generic simulation interface that abstracts out
most of the common parts of particle-based simulations.

`Simulation` uses callbacks to analyze and process simulation data on
the fly. The module `atooms.simulation.observers` provides basic
callbacks to write data to disk, e.g. trajejectory files, and to stop
the simulation when certain targets are reached, e.g. mean squared
displacement larger than a threshold.

The interval in steps at which callbacks are executed is controlled by
a `Scheduler` instance.

The actual simulation code is wrapped by a simulation "backend" that
exposes a minimal but coherent interface.
This file is part of atooms
Copyright 2010-2024, Daniele Coslovich

functions.name: atooms.simulation.core._report
documentation:
Log `info` string to default logger at level info.

Optionally write `info` to `file_handle` if the latter is
given. Logging is disabled via `log_echo` is False.

classes.name: atooms.simulation.core.Simulation

methods.name: atooms.simulation.core.Simulation.__init__
documentation:
Perform a simulation using the specified `backend` and optionally
write output to `output_path`. This can be a file or directory path.

Paths: to define output paths we rely on `output_path`, all
other paths are defined based on it and on its base_path.

methods.name: atooms.simulation.core.Simulation.restart
documentation: True is the simulation should be restarted. Read-only property.

methods.name: atooms.simulation.core.Simulation.add
documentation:
Add an observer `callback` to be called along with a `scheduler`.

`scheduler` and `callback` must be callables accepting a
Simulation instance as unique argument. `scheduler` must
return the next step at which the observer has to be notified.

An integer value is allowed for `scheduler`. In this case, a
scheduler with fixed interval is generated internally and the
observer is notified every `scheduler` steps.

documentation: Remove a callback.

If the checkpoint file is not found, this method exits
gracefully.

modules.name: atooms.backends
documentation:
Simulation backends.

A backend is an adapter to an actual implementation of a
simulation. The simulation backend interface must provide, at a
minimum, a `run()` method that performs the simulation. See the
`DryRun` backend for the interface that a valid backend should
implement.
This file is part of atooms
Copyright 2010-2024, Daniele Coslovich

documentation: Check whether system is polydisperse

classes.name: atooms.backends.f90.interaction.Interaction

methods.name: atooms.backends.f90.interaction.Interaction.__init__
atooms.backends.f90.interaction.Interaction.compute
documentation: Compute `observable` from this interaction

A fortran 90 backend to compute interactions between particles.

Note: we expect particle species to be either integers or
strings that can be cast to integers (corresponding either to 
`System.species_layout` equal to 'F' or 'C', but not to 'A'). 
If you encounter a `ValueError`, convert the species layout with
`system.species_layout = 'F'`.

Under the hoods, the idea is to stitch together four fortran modules:

1) a helpers module, which define common subroutines needed to
compute several types of interaction terms

2) a potential module, which defines subroutines to initialize and
compute a potential between particles

3) a cutoff module, which defines subroutines to initialize, cut
off and smooth a potential around some distance

4) an interaction module, which uses the above ones to compute the
total interaction energy, forces, etc.

The above modules are merged in a single source at run time and
compiled using f2py-jit.

functions.name: atooms.core.utils.tipify
documentation:
Convert a string into the best matching type.

Example:
-------
    2 -> int
    2.32 -> float
    text -> str

The only risk is if a variable is required to be float,
but is passed without dot.

Tests:
-----
    print type(tipify("2.0")) is float
    print type(tipify("2")) is int
    print type(tipify("t2")) is str
    print map(tipify, ["2.0", "2"])

classes.name: atooms.core.utils.OrderedSet
documentation:
Simple class to store an ordered set of items.

It does not try to reproduce either set or list interface. It just
provides a simple interface to deal with the set of distinct
chemical species in a system as it gets populated e.g. when
reading a trajectory. This covers the use case of grand-canonical
simulations.

Example:
-------

    particle = [Particle(species='A'), Particle(species='C')]
    periodic_table = OrderedSet()
    periodic_table.update([p.species for p in particle])
    particle = [Particle(species='A'), Particle(species='D')]
    periodic_table.update([p.species for p in particle])
    print periodic_table.index('C')
    print periodic_table[0]
    print periodic_table
